subtopics: Law of demand and demand shifters
Law of supply and supply shifters

glossary.definition: A schedule or curve showing the quantity of a good consumers are willing and able to purchase at each price, ceteris paribus; slopes downward due to the law of demand.
A schedule or curve showing the quantity producers are willing and able to sell at each price, ceteris paribus; slopes upward due to increasing marginal costs.
The price at which quantity demanded equals quantity supplied, clearing the market with no surplus or shortage.
A market condition in which quantity supplied exceeds quantity demanded at the prevailing price, placing downward pressure on price.
A market condition in which quantity demanded exceeds quantity supplied at the prevailing price, placing upward pressure on price.
A legally imposed maximum price; causes a shortage when set below equilibrium price.
A legally imposed minimum price; causes a surplus when set above equilibrium price.
The difference between the maximum a consumer is willing to pay and the price actually paid, representing welfare gain to buyers.
The difference between the price a producer receives and the minimum price at which the producer would supply the good.
Latin for 'all else equal'; the assumption that all variables other than those under study remain constant.
A good for which demand increases as consumer income rises.
A good for which demand decreases as consumer income rises.

canon.name: Market equilibrium condition
canon.form: Q_d(P) = Q_s(P) ⟹ solve for P*

canon.name: Total surplus
canon.form: TS = CS + PS

canon.name: Law of demand
canon.form: As price rises, quantity demanded falls, ceteris paribus (inverse relationship).

canon.name: Law of supply
canon.form: As price rises, quantity supplied rises, ceteris paribus (direct relationship).

canon.name: Demand shifters
canon.form: Demand shifts with changes in income, prices of related goods (substitutes/complements), tastes, expectations, and number of buyers.

canon.name: Supply shifters
canon.form: Supply shifts with changes in input prices, technology, expectations, number of sellers, and government taxes/subsidies.

canon.name: Deadweight loss from price control
canon.form: DWL = (1/2)|Q_free - Q_controlled|(|P_s - P_d|)

glossary.definition: The percentage change in quantity demanded divided by the percentage change in price; measures responsiveness of demand to price changes.
Demand is elastic when |PED| > 1; quantity demanded is highly responsive to price changes.
Demand is inelastic when |PED| < 1; quantity demanded is not very responsive to price changes.
Demand is unit elastic when |PED| = 1; total revenue is unchanged when price changes.
The percentage change in quantity demanded divided by the percentage change in income; positive for normal goods, negative for inferior goods.
The percentage change in quantity demanded of good X divided by the percentage change in price of good Y; positive for substitutes, negative for complements.
The percentage change in quantity supplied divided by the percentage change in price; always non-negative.
Supply or demand is perfectly inelastic when elasticity equals zero; the curve is vertical.
Supply or demand is perfectly elastic when elasticity is infinite; the curve is horizontal.
Elasticity calculated using midpoint (average) values of price and quantity over an interval, avoiding end-point sensitivity.

canon.name: Price elasticity of demand
canon.form: E_d = (ΔQ/Q) / (ΔP/P) = (ΔQ/ΔP)(P/Q)

canon.name: Income elasticity of demand
canon.form: YED = (%ΔQ_d) / (%ΔY)

canon.name: Cross-price elasticity
canon.form: XED = (%ΔQ_x) / (%ΔP_y)

canon.name: Total revenue and elasticity
canon.form: TR = P × Q; if |E_d| > 1, price ↑ ⟹ TR ↓; if |E_d| < 1, price ↑ ⟹ TR ↑

canon.name: Elasticity of linear demand curve
canon.form: E_d = -(b)(P/Q) where Q = a - bP

canon.name: Determinants of PED
canon.form: Demand is more elastic when: more close substitutes exist, the good is a luxury, it represents a large budget share, or the time horizon is longer.

canon.name: Tax incidence and elasticity
canon.form: The more inelastic side of the market bears a larger share of a tax burden.

glossary.definition: A numerical representation of a consumer's preferences over bundles of goods; higher utility indicates preferred bundles.
The additional utility gained from consuming one more unit of a good, holding other consumption constant; typically diminishing.
A curve showing all combinations of goods that yield equal utility; slopes downward and is convex to the origin under standard assumptions.
The rate at which a consumer is willing to trade one good for another while remaining on the same indifference curve; equals -MU_x/MU_y.
The set of consumption bundles affordable at given income and prices: P_x·X + P_y·Y = I.
Ordinary (uncompensated) demand that maximizes utility subject to the budget constraint; depends on prices and income.
Compensated demand that minimizes expenditure while achieving a given utility level; isolates the pure substitution effect.
The change in quantity demanded due solely to the change in relative prices, holding utility constant.
The change in quantity demanded due to the change in real purchasing power when a price changes.
Decomposes the total price effect into substitution and income effects: ∂x/∂p = ∂h/∂p - x·(∂x/∂I).
An inferior good with such a large income effect that demand curves upward; quantity demanded rises when price rises.
The minimum expenditure required to achieve a given utility level at given prices: e(p,u).
The maximum utility achievable given prices and income: V(p,I).

canon.name: Utility maximization (tangency)
canon.form: MU_x/P_x = MU_y/P_y  ⟺  MRS_{xy} = P_x/P_y

canon.name: Budget constraint
canon.form: P_x · X + P_y · Y = I

canon.name: Lagrangian for consumer problem
canon.form: max U(x,y) s.t. P_x·x + P_y·y = I; ℒ = U(x,y) - λ(P_x·x + P_y·y - I)

canon.name: Shephard's lemma
canon.form: x_i^h(p,u) = ∂e(p,u)/∂p_i

canon.name: MRS for Cobb-Douglas U = x^α·y^β
canon.form: MRS = (αy)/(βx)

canon.name: Law of diminishing marginal utility
canon.form: As consumption of a good increases, the marginal utility from each additional unit eventually declines.

canon.name: Expected utility (von Neumann-Morgenstern)
canon.form: EU = Σ π_i · U(w_i) where π_i is probability of outcome i

canon.name: Risk aversion condition
canon.form: A consumer is risk-averse iff U''(w) < 0 (concave utility function).

glossary.definition: A function Q = f(K,L) relating the quantity of output to the quantities of capital and labor (or other inputs) used.
The additional output produced by employing one more unit of labor, holding capital constant: MPL = ∂Q/∂L.
The rate at which capital can be substituted for labor while holding output constant; equals MPL/MPK along an isoquant.
A curve showing all combinations of inputs that produce a given quantity of output.
How output changes when all inputs are scaled proportionally: constant (CRS), increasing (IRS), or decreasing (DRS).
The sum of all costs incurred in production: TC = TFC + TVC.
Total cost per unit of output: ATC = TC/Q.
The incremental cost of producing one more unit of output: MC = ΔTC/ΔQ = dTC/dQ.
A cost already incurred and unrecoverable regardless of future decisions; should not influence forward-looking choices.
A situation in which long-run average cost declines as output increases.
A situation in which long-run average cost rises as output increases.
Cost savings achieved when it is cheaper to produce two goods jointly than separately: C(Q1,Q2) < C(Q1,0) + C(0,Q2).
A line showing all combinations of inputs that can be hired at a given total expenditure: wL + rK = C.

canon.name: Cost minimization condition
canon.form: MRTS = w/r  ⟺  MPL/MPK = w/r

canon.name: Isocost line
canon.form: wL + rK = C  ⟹  K = C/r - (w/r)L

canon.name: Cobb-Douglas production function
canon.form: Q = A·K^α·L^β; CRS if α+β=1, IRS if α+β>1, DRS if α+β<1

canon.name: Marginal product
canon.form: MPL = ∂Q/∂L;  MPK = ∂Q/∂K

canon.name: Profit maximization condition
canon.form: π = TR - TC;  dπ/dQ = 0  ⟹  MR = MC

canon.name: Short-run total cost
canon.form: STC(Q) = TFC + TVC(Q)

canon.name: Relationship between ATC and MC
canon.form: MC < ATC ⟹ ATC is falling; MC > ATC ⟹ ATC is rising; MC = ATC at minimum ATC.

canon.name: Long-run average cost envelope
canon.form: LRAC is the lower envelope of all SRAC curves; each SRAC is tangent to LRAC at one output level.

canon.name: Shephard's lemma (cost)
canon.form: Conditional input demand: x_i = ∂C(w,Q)/∂w_i

canon.name: Economies of scale index
canon.form: S = AC/MC; S>1 ⟹ economies of scale, S<1 ⟹ diseconomies

subtopics: Perfect competition: short-run and long-run equilibrium

glossary.definition: A market structure with many price-taking firms, homogeneous products, free entry/exit, and perfect information.
A firm that takes the market price as given because it is too small to influence it; faces a perfectly elastic demand curve.
A market with a single seller of a product with no close substitutes, giving the firm market power over price.
The ability of a firm to profitably set price above marginal cost.
A measure of market power: L = (P - MC)/P = -1/|E_d|; ranges from 0 (perfect competition) to 1 (pure monopoly).
Charging different prices to different customers for the same good, exploiting differences in willingness to pay.
An oligopoly model in which firms simultaneously choose quantities, taking rivals' quantities as fixed.
An oligopoly model in which firms simultaneously set prices; with homogeneous goods and equal costs, results in P = MC.
An oligopoly model in which a leader firm moves first, choosing quantity, and a follower then best-responds.
A market with many firms selling differentiated products, with free entry driving long-run profits to zero but P > MC.
A market in which a single firm can serve the entire demand at lower cost than two or more firms, due to economies of scale.
A measure of market concentration: HHI = Σ s_i^2 where s_i is each firm's market share in percentage; ranges from 0 to 10,000.

canon.name: Perfectly competitive firm's profit max
canon.form: P = MR = MC in short-run; P = MC = min AC in long-run

canon.name: Short-run supply curve (competitive firm)
canon.form: Firm supplies Q where P = MC(Q), for P ≥ min AVC; otherwise shuts down.

canon.name: Zero profit condition (long-run competition)
canon.form: Free entry/exit drives π → 0; P = min LRAC

canon.name: Deadweight loss from monopoly
canon.form: DWL = (1/2)(P_m - MC)(Q_c - Q_m)

canon.name: Cournot duopoly (symmetric, linear)
canon.form: a - bQ_1 - bQ_2 = MC; Best response: Q_i = (a-MC)/(2b) - Q_j/2; Nash: Q_i = (a-MC)/(3b)

canon.name: Bertrand paradox
canon.form: With homogeneous goods and equal costs, Bertrand competition yields P = MC even with only two firms.

canon.name: First-degree price discrimination
canon.form: Firm charges each consumer their maximum WTP; extracts all CS; output equals competitive level (no DWL).

canon.name: Third-degree price discrimination condition
canon.form: Optimal pricing across segments: MR_1 = MR_2 = MC; higher price in the less elastic segment.

subtopics: The fundamental theorems of welfare economics
Common resources and the tragedy of the commons

glossary.definition: An allocation is Pareto efficient if no one can be made better off without making someone else worse off.
A reallocation that makes at least one person better off without making anyone worse off.
A cost or benefit that affects a third party not involved in the market transaction, causing market failure.
A cost imposed on third parties by market participants; leads to overproduction relative to the social optimum.
A benefit conferred on third parties; leads to underproduction relative to the social optimum.
A corrective tax set equal to the marginal external cost, internalizing the externality and restoring social efficiency.
Private marginal cost plus marginal external cost: SMC = PMC + MEC.
If property rights are well-defined and transaction costs are zero, parties will negotiate to the efficient outcome regardless of the initial rights assignment.
A good that is both non-excludable (cannot prevent non-payers from consuming) and non-rival (one person's consumption does not reduce another's).
The tendency for individuals to under-contribute to public goods because they can benefit without paying.
A good that is non-excludable but rival in consumption; subject to overuse (tragedy of the commons).
The loss in total surplus from a market distortion (tax, externality, market power) relative to the efficient outcome.

canon.name: Social optimum condition with externality
canon.form: SMC = SMB; i.e., (PMC + MEC) = D(Q)

canon.name: Optimal Pigouvian tax
canon.form: t* = MEC evaluated at the socially optimal quantity Q*

canon.name: First fundamental theorem of welfare economics
canon.form: Every competitive equilibrium is Pareto efficient (given convexity and no externalities/public goods).

canon.name: Second fundamental theorem of welfare economics
canon.form: Any Pareto efficient allocation can be achieved as a competitive equilibrium after an appropriate lump-sum redistribution of endowments.

canon.name: Samuelson rule for public goods
canon.form: Σ MRS_i = MRT; sum of individual marginal rates of substitution equals the marginal rate of transformation.

canon.name: Coase theorem
canon.form: With zero transaction costs and well-defined property rights, bargaining achieves the efficient level of an externality regardless of who holds the rights.

canon.name: Deadweight loss formula
canon.form: DWL = (1/2)(P_s - P_d)(Q_free - Q_taxed)

subtopics: Normal form and extensive form games
Backward induction and subgame perfect equilibrium

glossary.definition: A strategy profile in which no player can increase their payoff by unilaterally deviating, given the strategies of all other players.
A strategy that yields a higher payoff than any other strategy regardless of what opponents play.
A probability distribution over pure strategies; a player randomizes to keep opponents indifferent.
A Nash equilibrium in which players' strategies constitute a Nash equilibrium in every subgame; found by backward induction.
A method for solving extensive-form games by starting at the terminal nodes and working backward to determine optimal choices.
A symmetric two-player game in which the dominant strategy for each player leads to a collectively suboptimal outcome.
A game in which players benefit from choosing matching strategies; typically has multiple Nash equilibria.
A Nash equilibrium that yields higher payoffs to all players than any other Nash equilibrium.
A game in which the same stage game is played multiple times; repetition can sustain cooperation via punishment strategies.
In infinitely repeated games with sufficient patience, any individually rational feasible outcome can be sustained as a subgame perfect equilibrium.
A Nash equilibrium in a game of incomplete information in which each player maximizes expected payoff given beliefs about types.
A Vickrey auction in which the highest bidder wins and pays the second-highest bid; truth-telling is a dominant strategy.

canon.name: Nash equilibrium definition
canon.form: s* is a NE iff for all i: u_i(s_i*, s_{-i}*) ≥ u_i(s_i, s_{-i}*) for all s_i ∈ S_i

canon.name: Mixed strategy indifference condition
canon.form: In a mixed NE, a player must be indifferent among all strategies played with positive probability.

canon.name: Prisoner's dilemma payoff structure
canon.form: T > R > P > S and R > (T+S)/2; defect is dominant for both, yielding (P,P) < (R,R).

canon.name: Subgame perfect equilibrium
canon.form: SPE requires Nash equilibrium strategies in every subgame, not only on-path; eliminates non-credible threats.

canon.name: Minimax theorem (zero-sum)
canon.form: max_{x} min_{y} u(x,y) = min_{y} max_{x} u(x,y); value of zero-sum game is unique.

canon.name: Grim trigger strategy (repeated games)
canon.form: Cooperate until opponent defects; then defect forever. Cooperation is sustained if δ ≥ (T-R)/(T-P).

canon.name: Revenue equivalence theorem
canon.form: Under standard conditions (symmetric independent private values, risk-neutral bidders), all standard auctions yield equal expected revenue.

subtopics: Nominal vs. real GDP and the GDP deflator
Limitations of GDP as a welfare measure
Price indices: CPI and GDP deflator

glossary.definition: The market value of all final goods and services produced within a country's borders in a given period.
GDP measured at current market prices without adjusting for inflation.
GDP adjusted for price changes using a base-year price level; reflects actual changes in output volume.
A price index equal to (Nominal GDP / Real GDP) × 100; measures the overall price level of domestically produced goods.
A price index measuring the cost of a fixed basket of goods and services purchased by a typical urban consumer.
The market value of all final goods and services produced by a country's residents, regardless of location: GNP = GDP + Net factor income from abroad.
The value of exports minus imports; a component of GDP equal to X - M.
The total income in the economy not spent on consumption or government purchases: S = Y - C - G.
Expenditure on new capital goods, residential construction, and changes in business inventories.
The increase in the value of a good or service at each stage of production; GDP equals total value added across all sectors.

canon.name: Expenditure approach to GDP
canon.form: GDP = C + I + G + (X - M)

canon.name: National income identity (closed economy)
canon.form: Y = C + I + G  ⟹  S = Y - C - G = I

canon.name: National income identity (open economy)
canon.form: S = I + NX  (or: S - I = NX = CA)

canon.name: Real GDP calculation
canon.form: Real GDP = Nominal GDP / GDP Deflator × 100

canon.name: GDP deflator
canon.form: GDP Deflator = (Nominal GDP / Real GDP) × 100

canon.name: GDP vs. GNP
canon.form: GNP = GDP + Net factor income from abroad (factor payments received from abroad minus factor payments made to foreigners)

canon.name: GDP as a welfare measure: limitations
canon.form: GDP omits household production, leisure, inequality, sustainability, and non-market activities; a rise in GDP does not necessarily imply a rise in welfare.

glossary.definition: The locus of (Y, r) pairs at which the goods market is in equilibrium (planned expenditure equals output); slopes downward in (Y, r) space.
The locus of (Y, r) pairs at which the money market is in equilibrium (money supply equals money demand); slopes upward in (Y, r) space.
The ratio by which output changes in response to an autonomous spending change: k = 1/(1-MPC) in a simple Keynesian model.
The reduction in private investment caused by rising interest rates when expansionary fiscal policy increases government borrowing.
A situation in which interest rates are at or near zero, making monetary policy ineffective because people prefer to hold cash rather than bonds.
The total demand for final goods and services in an economy at each price level; slopes downward due to the wealth, interest rate, and exchange rate effects.
The total supply of goods and services at each price level in the short run when nominal wages and prices of some inputs are sticky; slopes upward.
The total supply of goods and services at the natural rate of output (full employment); vertical in (Y, P) space.
The simultaneous occurrence of high inflation and high unemployment, typically caused by a negative supply shock.
The level of output consistent with full employment and the long-run equilibrium of the economy (potential GDP).
The fraction of an additional dollar of income that households spend on consumption.

canon.name: Goods market equilibrium (IS)
canon.form: Y = C(Y-T) + I(r) + G  ⟹  IS: r = f(Y) downward sloping

canon.name: Keynesian expenditure multiplier
canon.form: ΔY = ΔA / (1 - MPC) = k · ΔA; where k = 1/(1-MPC)

canon.name: Money market equilibrium (LM)
canon.form: M/P = L(Y, r);  L_Y > 0, L_r < 0  ⟹  LM: r = g(Y) upward sloping

canon.name: IS-LM simultaneous equilibrium
canon.form: Solve IS and LM jointly for equilibrium (Y*, r*).

canon.name: AD curve derivation
canon.form: AD slopes downward: higher P → lower M/P → higher r (LM shifts left) → lower I and Y.

canon.name: Tax multiplier
canon.form: ΔY = (-MPC/(1-MPC)) · ΔT; tax multiplier is negative and smaller in absolute value than spending multiplier.

canon.name: Balanced budget multiplier
canon.form: Equal increases in G and T yield ΔY = ΔG; the balanced budget multiplier equals 1.

canon.name: Long-run self-correction
canon.form: In ADAS model, a recessionary gap → falling wages → SRAS shifts right → Y returns to Y_n; no permanent output gap in long run.

subtopics: Money supply and the money multiplier
Central bank tools: reserve requirements, discount rate, open market operations
Fisher equation and real vs. nominal interest rates

glossary.definition: The most liquid measure of money including currency in circulation and demand deposits (checkable deposits).
A broader money measure including M1 plus savings deposits, small time deposits, and money market mutual funds.
The ratio of the money supply to the monetary base: m = 1/rr (in simple model) or m = (1+c)/(rr+c) with currency holdings.
Currency in circulation plus bank reserves; directly controlled by the central bank.
The fraction of deposits that banks hold as reserves; can be required (required reserve ratio) or held voluntarily (excess reserves).
Central bank purchases or sales of government securities to expand or contract the monetary base.
The interest rate expressed in monetary terms, unadjusted for inflation.
The nominal interest rate minus the inflation rate (or expected inflation): r ≈ i - π.
The theory that the price level is proportional to the money supply: MV = PY, implying inflation equals money growth minus output growth.
Revenue earned by the government from money creation; the real value of resources acquired by printing money.
A monetary policy guideline: i = r* + π + α(π - π*) + β(Y - Y*); the central bank raises interest rates when inflation exceeds target or output exceeds potential.
The average number of times a dollar is spent on final goods in a period: V = PY/M.

canon.name: Quantity equation
canon.form: M·V = P·Y  (MV = PQ)

canon.name: Quantity theory of inflation
canon.form: π = ΔM/M - ΔY/Y (assuming constant V)

canon.name: Fisher equation
canon.form: i = r + π^e  (nominal rate = real rate + expected inflation)

canon.name: Fisher effect
canon.form: A 1 percentage point increase in expected inflation raises the nominal interest rate by 1 percentage point, leaving the real rate unchanged.

canon.name: Money multiplier (simple)
canon.form: ΔDeposits = (1/rr) · ΔReserves;  m = 1/rr

canon.name: Money multiplier (with currency drain)
canon.form: m = (1 + c)/(rr + c)  where c = currency-to-deposit ratio

canon.name: Seigniorage
canon.form: Seigniorage = (ΔM/P) = (ΔM/M)·(M/P) = π·(M/P) in steady state

canon.name: Hyperinflation mechanism
canon.form: Large fiscal deficits monetized by central bank → rapid money growth → accelerating inflation → reduced real money demand → further price increases (Sargent-Wallace).

subtopics: Types of unemployment: frictional, structural, cyclical

glossary.definition: The percentage of the labor force that is not employed but actively seeking work: u = U/(U+E) × 100%.
The sum of employed and unemployed persons who are of working age and actively seeking employment.
Temporary unemployment arising from the time taken to match workers with suitable jobs; reflects normal labor market turnover.
Unemployment caused by a mismatch between workers' skills and the skills required by available jobs, often due to technological change or sectoral shifts.
Unemployment caused by insufficient aggregate demand during economic downturns; the gap between actual and natural unemployment.
The unemployment rate consistent with stable inflation; equal to frictional plus structural unemployment.
Non-Accelerating Inflation Rate of Unemployment; the unemployment rate at which inflation is stable; synonymous with natural rate in standard models.
An empirical inverse relationship between unemployment and inflation; the short-run Phillips curve shifts with expected inflation.
An empirical relationship between output gaps and cyclical unemployment: a 1 percentage point rise in unemployment corresponds to approximately a 2% fall in output below potential.
A wage above the market-clearing level set by firms to boost worker productivity, reduce turnover, or attract better workers; can cause unemployment.
The percentage of the civilian non-institutional population that is in the labor force.

canon.name: Unemployment rate
canon.form: u = U / (U + E) = U / LF

canon.name: Labor force participation rate
canon.form: LFPR = LF / Working-age population

canon.name: Short-run Phillips curve
canon.form: π = π^e - α(u - u*);  inflation rises when u < u*

canon.name: Expectations-augmented Phillips curve
canon.form: π = π^e + β(Y - Y*) - α(u - u*);  adaptive expectations: π^e = π_{t-1}

canon.name: Long-run Phillips curve
canon.form: Vertical at u = u* (NAIRU); in long run, expected inflation = actual inflation, no trade-off between π and u.

canon.name: Steady-state unemployment (flows)
canon.form: u* = s / (s + f);  s = job separation rate, f = job finding rate

canon.name: Sacrifice ratio
canon.form: Sacrifice ratio = (cumulative % output loss) / (percentage point reduction in inflation); measures cost of disinflation.

subtopics: Total factor productivity and the Solow residual

glossary.definition: A neoclassical growth model in which the long-run growth rate is determined by exogenous technological progress; capital accumulation drives transitional dynamics.
The capital-labor ratio: k = K/L; the central state variable in the Solow model.
The long-run equilibrium in the Solow model where capital per worker is constant (investment equals depreciation plus population growth).
The rate at which capital wears out or becomes obsolete, typically denoted δ.
The steady-state capital-per-worker level that maximizes consumption per worker: MPK = δ + n + g.
The portion of output growth not explained by input growth; a measure of technological efficiency: A in Y = A·F(K,L).
The empirical measure of TFP growth, calculated as output growth minus the weighted sum of capital and labor growth.
The tendency for economies with similar structural parameters to converge to the same steady state; poorer economies grow faster given identical fundamentals.
Growth theory in which the long-run growth rate is explained by the model itself (e.g., through R&D, human capital, or knowledge spillovers) rather than exogenous TFP.
A technique decomposing output growth into contributions from capital, labor, and TFP: ΔY/Y = α·ΔK/K + (1-α)·ΔL/L + ΔA/A.

canon.name: Solow production function (intensive form)
canon.form: y = f(k) = A·k^α  (Cobb-Douglas); where y = Y/L, k = K/L

canon.name: Capital accumulation equation
canon.form: Δk = s·f(k) - (δ + n + g)·k

canon.name: Steady-state condition
canon.form: s·f(k*) = (δ + n + g)·k*  ⟹  k* is the steady-state capital per effective worker

canon.name: Golden rule condition
canon.form: MPK = δ + n + g  ⟺  α·f(k_GR)/k_GR = δ + n + g

canon.name: Growth accounting (Solow residual)
canon.form: ΔA/A = ΔY/Y - α·ΔK/K - (1-α)·ΔL/L

canon.name: Long-run growth rate
canon.form: In Solow model, long-run growth of Y per worker = g (rate of technological progress); growth of Y = g + n.

canon.name: Convergence prediction
canon.form: Economies with lower k relative to k* grow faster; speed of convergence ≈ (1-α)(δ+n+g).

canon.name: AK (endogenous) growth model
canon.form: Y = AK; constant MPK → no diminishing returns → sustained growth without exogenous TFP.

subtopics: Fiscal policy: discretionary and automatic stabilizers
Government budget constraint and debt dynamics
Crowding out and fiscal multiplier in open economy
Zero lower bound and unconventional monetary policy

glossary.definition: Deliberate changes in government spending or taxes to stabilize the economy, requiring explicit policy decisions.
Budget mechanisms that automatically increase spending or reduce taxes in recessions (e.g., unemployment insurance, progressive tax) without new legislation.
The proposition that tax cuts financed by debt have no effect on aggregate demand because rational consumers save the tax cut in anticipation of future higher taxes.
G - T = ΔB + ΔM; the government deficit must be financed by issuing bonds or money.
The government deficit excluding interest payments on existing debt: G - T (excluding interest).
The change in the debt-to-GDP ratio depends on the primary deficit and the difference between the real interest rate and the growth rate.
A monetary policy framework in which the central bank announces an explicit inflation target and adjusts policy instruments to achieve it.
The tendency for policymakers to deviate from previously announced optimal plans once the private sector has formed expectations; motivates central bank independence and rules.
An unconventional monetary policy in which the central bank purchases large quantities of assets (beyond short-term government bonds) to lower long-term interest rates and stimulate the economy.
The reduction in private investment resulting from government borrowing that raises interest rates; reduces the effectiveness of fiscal expansion.

canon.name: Government budget constraint
canon.form: G + r·B = T + ΔB + ΔM/P  (spending + interest = revenue + bond issuance + seigniorage)

canon.name: Debt-to-GDP dynamics
canon.form: Δ(B/Y) = (G-T)/Y + (r-g)·(B/Y);  debt ratio rises if r > g and primary deficit > 0

canon.name: Fiscal multiplier (closed economy, simple)
canon.form: ΔY = ΔG/(1-MPC);  or  ΔY = (-MPC/(1-MPC))·ΔT

canon.name: Ricardian equivalence condition
canon.form: If consumers are forward-looking and face perfect capital markets, a bond-financed tax cut does not affect consumption: ΔC = 0.

canon.name: Taylor rule
canon.form: i_t = r* + π_t + α(π_t - π*) + β(Y_t - Y_t*)/Y_t*;  typically α=0.5, β=0.5

canon.name: Monetary policy transmission channels
canon.form: Interest rate channel → investment; credit channel → bank lending; exchange rate channel → net exports; asset price channel → wealth/consumption.

canon.name: Zero lower bound constraint
canon.form: i ≥ 0; when i = 0, conventional monetary policy loses effectiveness; QE, forward guidance, and negative rates are alternatives.

canon.name: Barro-Ricardo tax smoothing
canon.form: Optimally, taxes should be smoothed over time (like permanent income); temporary spending increases should be debt-financed.

subtopics: Measures of fit: R², adjusted R²
Serial correlation and Newey-West standard errors

glossary.definition: A method of estimating regression coefficients by minimizing the sum of squared residuals.
The estimated change in the dependent variable associated with a one-unit increase in an independent variable, holding other variables constant.
The difference between the observed and fitted value of the dependent variable: e_i = y_i - ŷ_i.
The proportion of variance in the dependent variable explained by the regression: R² = 1 - SSR/SST; ranges from 0 to 1.
R² adjusted for the number of regressors: R̄² = 1 - [(1-R²)(n-1)/(n-k-1)]; penalizes for additional variables.
The ratio of an estimated coefficient to its standard error: t = β̂/SE(β̂); used to test whether a coefficient is statistically different from a hypothesized value.
A test statistic for the joint significance of multiple coefficients; tests whether all slope coefficients are simultaneously zero.
The probability of observing a test statistic at least as extreme as the one calculated, under the null hypothesis; a small p-value (< 0.05) rejects the null.
Bias in OLS estimates when a variable that affects the dependent variable and is correlated with included regressors is excluded from the regression.
A violation of OLS assumption in which the variance of the error term varies across observations; OLS remains unbiased but is no longer efficient, and standard errors are incorrect.
A condition in which two or more independent variables are highly correlated, making it difficult to estimate their individual coefficients precisely.
Under classical OLS assumptions (linearity, exogeneity, homoskedasticity, no serial correlation), OLS is BLUE: Best Linear Unbiased Estimator.
The estimated standard deviation of an estimator; measures the precision of the coefficient estimate.

canon.name: OLS estimator (simple regression)
canon.form: β̂_1 = Σ(x_i - x̄)(y_i - ȳ) / Σ(x_i - x̄)²  =  Cov(x,y)/Var(x)

canon.name: Decomposition of variance
canon.form: SST = SSE + SSR;  R² = SSE/SST = 1 - SSR/SST

canon.name: t-test for a coefficient
canon.form: H_0: β_k = 0;  t = β̂_k / SE(β̂_k) ~ t_{n-k-1} under H_0

canon.name: F-test for joint significance
canon.form: F = [(SSR_R - SSR_U)/q] / [SSR_U/(n-k-1)] ~ F_{q, n-k-1}

canon.name: Omitted variable bias formula
canon.form: plim(β̂_1) = β_1 + β_2·δ_{21}; where δ_{21} = Cov(x_2,x_1)/Var(x_1) (from auxiliary regression x_2 on x_1)

canon.name: Gauss-Markov theorem
canon.form: Under assumptions: (1) linearity, (2) random sampling, (3) no perfect collinearity, (4) E[u|X]=0, (5) homoskedasticity; OLS is BLUE.

canon.name: White's heteroskedasticity-consistent SE
canon.form: Use robust standard errors: V̂(β̂) = (X'X)^{-1}(Σ ê_i²·x_i·x_i')(X'X)^{-1} when Var(u_i|x_i) ≠ σ².

canon.name: Log-log interpretation
canon.form: ln(y) = β_0 + β_1·ln(x): β_1 is the elasticity of y with respect to x.

canon.name: Log-linear interpretation
canon.form: ln(y) = β_0 + β_1·x: β_1 × 100 is the approximate % change in y per unit change in x.

topics.topic: Econometrics: Causal Inference and Advanced Methods
subtopics: Panel data: fixed effects and random effects
Weak instruments and the first-stage F-test

glossary.definition: A condition in which a regressor is correlated with the error term, causing OLS to be biased and inconsistent; arises from omitted variables, reverse causality, or measurement error.
A variable that is correlated with an endogenous regressor (relevance) but uncorrelated with the error term (exclusion restriction); used to obtain consistent estimates.
An IV estimation procedure: first regress the endogenous variable on instruments and exogenous controls; then use fitted values in the second-stage regression.
The identifying assumption that an instrument affects the outcome only through the endogenous variable of interest, not directly.
A quasi-experimental method comparing changes in outcomes over time between a treatment group and a control group to identify causal effects.
An identification strategy exploiting a known discontinuity in treatment assignment at a threshold of a running variable.
A panel data estimator that removes time-invariant unobserved heterogeneity by demeaning within each unit.
A panel data estimator that treats the unit-level unobserved effects as random and uncorrelated with regressors; more efficient than FE if the assumption holds.
A specification test comparing FE and RE estimates; a significant difference indicates correlation between unobserved effects and regressors, favoring FE.
The average treatment effect for compliers — units whose treatment status is changed by the instrument; the IV estimand under heterogeneous treatment effects.
An individual whose treatment status changes in the same direction as the instrument; IV identifies the treatment effect for compliers only.
Instruments with low correlation with the endogenous variable, leading to biased IV estimates and invalid inference; diagnosed by first-stage F-statistic (rule of thumb: F > 10).
The identifying assumption for DiD: absent the treatment, the treated and control groups would have followed the same trend in outcomes.

canon.name: IV estimator (just-identified)
canon.form: β̂_IV = (Z'X)^{-1}Z'y  =  Cov(Z,Y)/Cov(Z,X)

canon.name: 2SLS procedure
canon.form: Stage 1: X̂ = Z(Z'Z)^{-1}Z'X;  Stage 2: β̂_2SLS = (X̂'X)^{-1}X̂'y

canon.name: IV consistency condition
canon.form: Cov(Z, u) = 0 (exclusion) and Cov(Z, X) ≠ 0 (relevance)

canon.name: DiD estimator
canon.form: β_DiD = (Ȳ_{treat,post} - Ȳ_{treat,pre}) - (Ȳ_{control,post} - Ȳ_{control,pre})

canon.name: DiD regression form
canon.form: Y_{it} = α + β·Treat_i + γ·Post_t + δ·(Treat_i × Post_t) + ε_{it};  δ = DiD estimate

canon.name: Sharp RDD estimand
canon.form: τ_RDD = lim_{x→c+} E[Y|X=x] - lim_{x→c-} E[Y|X=x];  discontinuity in E[Y|X] at cutoff c

canon.name: Fixed effects estimator (within)
canon.form: β̂_FE = (X̃'X̃)^{-1}X̃'Ỹ  where X̃_{it} = X_{it} - X̄_i (within-group demeaning)

canon.name: Hausman test statistic
canon.form: H = (β̂_FE - β̂_RE)'[Var(β̂_FE) - Var(β̂_RE)]^{-1}(β̂_FE - β̂_RE) ~ χ²_k under H_0 (RE consistent)

canon.name: LATE theorem (Imbens-Angrist)
canon.form: IV estimand = E[Y_1 - Y_0 | complier] = LATE; identifies effect only for those whose treatment is changed by the instrument.

canon.name: Weak instrument rule of thumb
canon.form: First-stage F-statistic > 10 (Staiger-Stock); or use Stock-Yogo critical values for size distortion.
